result.markdown:
# Logistic regression (for binary data) {#sec-logistic} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: When our response variable is binary, we can use a glm with a **binomial** error distribution So far we have only considered continuous and discrete data as response variables. What if our response is a categorical variable (e.g passing or failing an exam, voting yes or no in a referendum, whether an egg has successfully fledged or been predated, infected/uninfected, alive/dead)? We can model the **probability** $p$ of being in a particular class as a function of other explanatory variables. These type of **binary** data are assumed to follow a **Bernoulli** distribution (which is a special case of *Binomial*) which has the following characteristics: $$ Y \sim \mathcal{Bern}(p) $$ * **Binary** variable, taking the values 0 or 1 (yes/no, pass/fail). * A **probability** parameter $p$, where $0 < p < 1$. * **Mean** = $p$ * **Variance** = $p(1 - p)$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](binomial_files/figure-html/bernplot-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: Let us now place the Gaussian (simple linear regression), and logistic models next to each other: $$ \begin{aligned} Y & \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) &&& Y \sim \mathcal{Bern}(p)\\ \mu & = \beta_0 + \beta_1X &&& ?? = \beta_0 + \beta_1X \end{aligned} $$ Now we need to fill in the `??` with the appropriate term. We cannot simply model the probability as $p = \beta_0 + \beta_1X$, because $p$ **cannot** be negative. $\log{p} = \beta_0 + \beta_1X$ won't work either, because $p$ cannot be greater than 1. Instead we model the **log odds** $\log\left(\frac{p}{1 - p}\right)$ as a linear function. So our logistic regression model looks like this: $$ \begin{aligned} Y & \sim \mathcal{Bern}(p)\\ \log\left(\frac{p}{1 - p}\right) & = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X \end{aligned} $$ Again, note that we are still "only" fitting straight lines through our data, but this time in the log odds space. As a shorthand notation we write $\log\left(\frac{p}{1 - p}\right) = \text{logit}(p) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](binomial_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: We can also re-arrange the above equation so that we get an expression for $p$ $$ p = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}} $$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](binomial_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: Note how $p$ can only vary between 0 and 1. To implement the logistic regression model in R, we choose `family=binomial(link=logit)` (the Bernoulli distribution is a special case of the Binomial distribution). ``` glm(response ~ explanatory, family=binomial(link=logit)) ``` ## Example: Dosage response to pesticides ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](images/Tribolium castaneum.jpg){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: This small dataset is from an experiment looking at mortality in batches of flour exposed to different doses of pesticides. The question of interest is: > How does pesticide dose affect mortality in the flour beetle *Tribolium castaneum* ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} head(beetles) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Dose| Number_tested| Number_killed| Mortality_rate| |-----:|-------------:|-------------:|--------------:| | 49.06| 49| 6| 0.1224490| | 52.99| 60| 13| 0.2166667| | 56.91| 62| 18| 0.2903226| | 60.84| 56| 28| 0.5000000| | 64.76| 63| 52| 0.8253968| | 68.69| 59| 53| 0.8983051|
::: ::: The data columns are: - **Dose**: Pesticide dosage (mg) - **Number_tested** - Number of beetles in application - **Number_killed**: Number of beetles that died - **Mortality_rate** Calculated proportion of deaths ## Data tidying A binomial model calculates the numbers of `wins/losses` out of a series of trials, here that will refer to beetles "successfully killed" by the pesticide/ survivors. The GLM of the binomial counts will analyse the number of *each* batch of beetles killed, while taking into account the size of each group :::{.task} ::::{.task-header} Your turn :::: ::::{.task-container} * [ ] Clean the names in the dataset * [ ] Use `mutate` to make a new column `number_survived` from the beetles not killed in each trial * [ ] Include any sensible data checks
::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} beetles <- beetles |> janitor::clean_names() |> # clean names mutate(number_survived = number_tested-number_killed) # mutate ``` :::
:::: ::: :::{.callout-note collapse = "true"} ## A linear model Here we can fit a standard linear model to calculated proportions. This shows issues with normality and linearity ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} performance::check_model(model, detrend = FALSE) ``` ::: Predictions made from this model are also inadequate ::: ## Fitting a binomial model ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} binomial_model <- glm(cbind(number_killed,number_survived) ~ dose, family = binomial, data = beetles) ``` ::: :::{.panel-tabset} ## summary ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} summary(binomial_model) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: glm(formula = cbind(number_killed, number_survived) ~ dose, family = binomial, data = beetles) Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) (Intercept) -14.57806 1.29846 -11.23 <2e-16 *** dose 0.24554 0.02149 11.42 <2e-16 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) Null deviance: 267.6624 on 7 degrees of freedom Residual deviance: 8.4379 on 6 degrees of freedom AIC: 38.613 Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4 ``` ::: ::: ## broom ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} binomial_model |> broom::tidy(conf.int=T) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|term | estimate| std.error| statistic| p.value| conf.low| conf.high| |:-----------|-----------:|---------:|---------:|-------:|-----------:|-----------:| |(Intercept) | -14.5780604| 1.2984622| -11.22717| 0| -17.2645230| -12.1608424| |dose | 0.2455399| 0.0214937| 11.42381| 0| 0.2056099| 0.2900912|
::: ::: ::: So we are now fitting the following model $$ Y \sim Bern(p) $$ $$ \log\left[ \frac { P( \operatorname{oring\_binary} = fail) }{ 1 - P( \operatorname{oring\_binary} = fail) } \right] = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}(\operatorname{temp}) $$ Which in R will look like this: - Intercept = $\beta_{0}$ = -14.5 When pesticide dose is zero the mean *log-odds* are -14.5 [95%CI: -17.2 - -12.1] for a death in beetles - dose = $\beta_{1}$ = 0.24 [95%CI: 0.2 - 0.29] For every rise in the pesticide dose by 1mg, the *log-odds* of a death increase by 0.24. ### Interpreting model coefficients We first consider why we are dealing with odds $\frac{p}{1-p}$ instead of just $p$. They contain the same information, so the choice is somewhat arbitrary, however we’ve been using probabilities for so long that it feels unnatural to switch to odds. There are good reasons for this, however. Odds $\frac{p}{1-p}$ can take on any value from 0 to ∞ and so part of our translation of $p$ to an unrestricted domain is already done ($P$ is restricted to 0-1). Take a look at some examples below: +----------------+-------------------------------------------+------------------------+ | Probability | Odds | Verbiage | +================+===========================================+========================+ | $p=.95$ | $\frac{95}{5} = \frac{19}{1} = 19$ | 19 to 1 odds for | +----------------+-------------------------------------------+------------------------+ | $p=.75$ | $\frac{75}{25} = \frac{3}{1} = 3$ | 3 to 1 odds for | +----------------+-------------------------------------------+------------------------+ | $p=.50$ | $\frac{50}{50} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$ | 1 to 1 odds | +----------------+-------------------------------------------+------------------------+ | $p=.25$ | $\frac{25}{75} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.33$ | 3 to 1 odds against | +----------------+-------------------------------------------+------------------------+ | $p=.05$ | $\frac{95}{5} = \frac{1}{19} = 0.0526$ | 19 to 1 odds against | +----------------+-------------------------------------------+------------------------+ When we use a binomial model, we produce the 'log-odds', this produces a fully unconstrained linear regression as anything less than 1, can now occupy an infinite negative value -∞ to ∞. $$ \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_{0}+\beta_{1}x_{1}+\beta_{2}x_{2} $$ $$ \frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\beta_{0}}e^{\beta_{1}x_{1}}e^{\beta_{2}x_{2}} $$ We can interpret $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$ as the increase in the log odds for every unit increase in $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$. We could alternatively interpret $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$ using the notion that a one unit change in $x_{1}$ as a percent change of $e^{\beta_{1}}$ in the odds. That is to say, $e^{\beta_{1}}$ is the odds ratio of that change. If you want to find out more about [Odds and log-odds head here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8nm0G-1uJzA) ## Probability ### Making predictions :::{.panel-tabset} ## Augment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} binomial_model |> broom::augment(data = beetles, type.predict = "response") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| dose| number_tested| number_killed| mortality_rate| number_survived| .fitted| .resid| .hat| .sigma| .cooksd| .std.resid| |-----:|-------------:|-------------:|--------------:|---------------:|---------:|----------:|---------:|--------:|---------:|----------:| | 49.06| 49| 6| 0.1224490| 43| 0.0736536| 1.2025499| 0.2458459| 1.141959| 0.3695718| 1.3847554| | 52.99| 60| 13| 0.2166667| 47| 0.1726583| 0.8747551| 0.3477614| 1.205381| 0.3324978| 1.0831366| | 56.91| 62| 18| 0.2903226| 44| 0.3533406| -1.0542088| 0.3104417| 1.168435| 0.3517707| -1.2695246| | 60.84| 56| 28| 0.5000000| 28| 0.5891818| -1.3455544| 0.2366298| 1.101468| 0.3736036| -1.5400459| | 64.76| 63| 52| 0.8253968| 11| 0.7896971| 0.7110658| 0.2848855| 1.243451| 0.1346649| 0.8408568| | 68.69| 59| 53| 0.8983051| 6| 0.9078843| -0.2506399| 0.2530093| 1.292579| 0.0146767| -0.2899964| | 72.61| 62| 61| 0.9838710| 1| 0.9626942| 0.9887023| 0.1992421| 1.201427| 0.1202801| 1.1048796| | 76.54| 60| 60| 1.0000000| 0| 0.9854508| 1.3261731| 0.1221842| 1.134404| 0.0702320| 1.4154630|
::: ::: ## Emmeans ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} emmeans::emmeans(binomial_model, specs = ~ dose, at=list(dose=c(49:77)), type='response') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` dose prob SE df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL 49 0.0727 0.01830 Inf 0.0439 0.118 50 0.0910 0.02100 Inf 0.0574 0.141 51 0.1135 0.02370 Inf 0.0747 0.169 52 0.1406 0.02640 Inf 0.0965 0.201 53 0.1730 0.02880 Inf 0.1236 0.237 54 0.2110 0.03090 Inf 0.1568 0.278 55 0.2548 0.03250 Inf 0.1965 0.323 56 0.3041 0.03340 Inf 0.2428 0.373 57 0.3584 0.03380 Inf 0.2951 0.427 58 0.4166 0.03370 Inf 0.3524 0.484 59 0.4772 0.03330 Inf 0.4128 0.542 60 0.5385 0.03260 Inf 0.4743 0.601 61 0.5987 0.03190 Inf 0.5349 0.659 62 0.6560 0.03100 Inf 0.5930 0.714 63 0.7091 0.03000 Inf 0.6471 0.764 64 0.7570 0.02860 Inf 0.6967 0.809 65 0.7993 0.02700 Inf 0.7412 0.847 66 0.8358 0.02500 Inf 0.7808 0.879 67 0.8668 0.02290 Inf 0.8153 0.906 68 0.8927 0.02050 Inf 0.8453 0.927 69 0.9141 0.01820 Inf 0.8710 0.944 70 0.9315 0.01600 Inf 0.8928 0.957 71 0.9456 0.01380 Inf 0.9113 0.967 72 0.9569 0.01180 Inf 0.9267 0.975 73 0.9660 0.01010 Inf 0.9396 0.981 74 0.9732 0.00851 Inf 0.9504 0.986 75 0.9789 0.00715 Inf 0.9593 0.989 76 0.9834 0.00597 Inf 0.9666 0.992 77 0.9870 0.00496 Inf 0.9727 0.994 Confidence level used: 0.95 Intervals are back-transformed from the logit scale ``` ::: ::: ::: :::{.task} ::::{.task-header} Make a ggplot of the change in probability :::: ::::{.task-container} - Take one of your model predictions - If emmeans convert to `as_tibble()` first - Plot this on a ggplot - Include 95% confidence intervals (if possible)
:::{.panel-tabset} ## Broom ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} binomial_model |> broom::augment(data = beetles, type.predict = "response", se_fit = TRUE) |> ggplot(aes(x=dose, y=mortality_rate))+ geom_point()+ geom_line(aes( x = dose, y = .fitted))+ geom_ribbon(aes(ymin=.fitted - (1.96 * .se.fit), ymax= .fitted + (1.96 * .se.fit)), alpha=0.2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](binomial_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Emmeans ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} emmeans::emmeans(binomial_model, specs = ~ dose, at=list(dose=c(40:80)), type='response') |> as_tibble() |> ggplot(aes(x=dose, y=prob))+ geom_point(data = beetles, aes(x = dose, y = mortality_rate))+ geom_line(aes(x=dose, y=prob))+ geom_ribbon(aes(ymin=asymp.LCL, ymax=asymp.UCL), alpha=0.2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](binomial_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: :::
:::: ::: :::{.task} ::::{.task-header} Prediction :::: ::::{.task-container} Can you edit your code to produce a prediction of the mortality rate at a pesticide dose of 0mg?
::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} emmeans::emmeans(binomial_model, specs = ~ dose, at=list(dose=0), type='response') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` dose prob SE df asymp.LCL asymp.UCL 0 4.66e-07 6.06e-07 Inf 3.66e-08 5.94e-06 Confidence level used: 0.95 Intervals are back-transformed from the logit scale ``` ::: :::
:::: ::: ## Assumptions The `performance` package detects and alters the checks. Pay particular attention to the binned residuals plot - this is your best estimate of possible overdispersion (requiring a quasi-likelihood fit). In this case there is likely not enough data for robust checks - we could consider a quasi-likelihood model in order to have more conservative confidence intervals. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} performance::check_model(binomial_model, detrend = FALSE) ``` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} performance::check_overdispersion(binomial_model) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # Overdispersion test dispersion ratio = 1.002 p-value = 0.992 ``` ::: ::: :::{.task} ::::{.task-header} Try fitting a quasi-likelihood model :::: ::::{.task-container} - Fit a quasibinomial model ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} quasibinomial_model <- glm(cbind(number_killed,number_survived) ~ dose, family = quasibinomial, data = beetles) ``` ::: - How does this change the means, standard errors and significance tests - Do you think we need it? :::: ::: ## Write-ups :::{.task} ::::{.task-header} Write up the results :::: ::::{.task-container} - How would you write up for findings from this analysis?
Mortality rate increased with pesticide dose in the red flour beetle (*Tribolium castaneum*). Across the observed pesticide dosage each mg of pesticide was associated with a .25 [95%CI: 0.21 - 0.29] increase in the log odds of mortality (z = 11.42, P < 0.001). At the lowest pesticide dose (49mg) we observed a mean mortality of 7% [4.4 - 11.8%], at the middle range (60mg) we observed a mean mortality of 53.9% [47.4 - 90%] and at the highest dosage (77mg) we saw almost total mortality 98.7%[97.2 - 99.4%].
:::: ::: ## Binomial vs Linear Model :::{.callout-note collapse = "true"} ## Calculating R squared Binomial model shows better explanatory power (R-squared) for our correctly fitted model: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} DescTools::PseudoR2(binomial_model) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` McFadden 0.9979834 ``` ::: ::: :::
